lesson.menu_desc: Relative Clauses in Cross-Cultural Negotiations -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 11 -- Reading the Room -- Relative Clauses in Cross-Cultural Negotiations
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Fabiana Michelly Silva | Aula 11 -- Reading the Room

listenings.text: Good morning. Before tomorrow's call with our Japanese partners, let me give you a quick cultural briefing. Japan is what we call a high-context culture, which means a great deal of meaning is communicated indirectly. The people who lead these negotiations rarely say no directly. A response that sounds like maybe is often a polite refusal, which is something you have to read between the lines. Silence, which many Westerners find uncomfortable, is actually a sign of careful thinking. The senior manager, whose approval we need, will speak last, so do not push for a quick decision. Etiquette matters enormously here. The colleague who introduces you sets the tone, so let them lead. And remember the concept of saving face: never put anyone in a position where they look wrong in front of their team. The deal that respects these unwritten rules is the deal that closes. Build common ground first, and the contract will follow.

Hi team, here is my debrief after the negotiation with our German counterparts, which went very differently from what some of us expected. Germany is a low-context culture, which means directness is valued, not seen as rude. The buyer who led the meeting got straight to the numbers, which surprised Carlos, who is used to more small talk. There was very little of the relationship-building that we do in Brazil. The point that I want to emphasize is this: directness there is a sign of respect, not aggression. The manager whose team handles procurement asked very pointed questions, which is normal. The mediator who joined us helped us adjust our pace. What I learned is that the negotiator who reads the room, and who adapts their style to the culture in front of them, is the one who builds trust. Reciprocity also matters: when they made a concession, we were expected to offer one in return.

extra_audio.text: Marcus gives the team a cultural briefing before the call. He explains that Japan is a high-context culture where meaning is indirect. He warns the team to read between the lines and respect the idea of saving face. Fabiana leads the call and lets the local colleague introduce everyone first. The team builds common ground before discussing the numbers, and the deal closes successfully.
